metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Mirror Exp A's retry-cap policy in Exp B: specify a maximum retry count (e.g. 3) per permutation for infra-INDETERMINATE re-runs, and define that a permutation exhausting its retries is recorded as an excluded row in a separate `e6_ordershuffle_excluded.jsonl` (not silently padded), with the AC's proving script updated to tolerate the excluded count.
evidence: Exp A explicitly: 'capping retries and recording any finding that cannot reach 3 votes as an excluded, reported row (never silently padded)'
Exp B only: 're-run a permutation whose INDETERMINATE carries coverage.llm_unavailable/verify_failed' — no cap, no fallback, no exclusion policy
ABSENCE: no maximum retry count for Exp B permutations; no specification of what to write to e6_ordershuffle.jsonl if all retries are exhausted
findings.finding: Exp B's retry/exclusion cap for infra-INDETERMINATE permutations is undefined, unlike Exp A which explicitly states 'capping retries and recording any finding that cannot reach 3 votes as an excluded, reported row (never silently padded)'. Exp B only says 're-run a permutation whose INDETERMINATE carries coverage.llm_unavailable/verify_failed' with no cap on re-tries and no defined failure mode if a permutation persistently returns infra-INDETERMINATE.
findings.location: Experiment B — 'Record the resulting verdict, applying the infra-INDETERMINATE exclusion defined above (re-run a permutation whose INDETERMINATE carries coverage.llm_unavailable/verify_failed)'
scenarios: A persistent LLM-tier outage causes every re-run of a permutation to return infra-INDETERMINATE; the harness loops indefinitely or skips the permutation silently, corrupting the 14×3 count and the κ calculation

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the infra-exclusion rule to also re-run permutations where `coverage.get('verify_failed')` is set AND the verdict is PASS — not just INDETERMINATE — because `orchestrator.py:493-501` shows `verify_failed` can produce PASS for advisory-only plans.
evidence: Plan states: 'an INDETERMINATE carrying `coverage.get("verify_failed")` ... is an execution failure, not a stable judge outcome — E6 drops and re-runs'
orchestrator.py:493-501: `verdict = 'INDETERMINATE' if _any_blocking_criterion(indeterminate) else 'PASS'` — verify_failed produces PASS when no blocking criterion is involved
three_pass.py:283: `pass3_decide(verify, block_threshold=0.95, blocking_enabled=False)` — default is advisory-only, so `_any_blocking_criterion` returns False for typical corpus_sample plans
The exclusion rule only triggers on INDETERMINATE verdicts; a verify_failed+PASS verdict passes through silently as a valid data point
findings.finding: The plan's infra-INDETERMINATE exclusion rule for Exp B states it re-runs any permutation whose verdict is INDETERMINATE with `coverage.verify_failed` set. However, `orchestrator.py:493-501` shows that `verify_failed` can produce PASS (not INDETERMINATE) when no blocking-enabled criterion is involved: `verdict = 'INDETERMINATE' if _any_blocking_criterion(indeterminate) else 'PASS'`. Plans in `corpus_sample.json` are typically non-blocking (all LLM criteria default to advisory posture per `three_pass.py`'s `blocking_enabled=False` default), so a `verify_failed` event on these plans will produce PASS, not INDETERMINATE — bypassing the re-run rule and silently counting a compromised run as a stable PASS. The plan's exclusion definition ("Grounded definitions" section and Exp B mechanism) does not account for this conditional and claims `verify_failed` is an infra failure that cannot produce a stable PASS.
findings.location: Exp B mechanism / "Grounded definitions" — infra-INDETERMINATE exclusion; orchestrator.py:493-501
scenarios: Agentic verifier exhausts step budget on one permutation of a non-blocking plan — finalize_verdict returns PASS (not INDETERMINATE) with verify_failed set — the run is counted as a stable PASS verdict in the agreement table, inflating κ and raw agreement with a contaminated data point
findings.suggested_fix: In the Exp B infra-exclusion check, add: 'Also re-run (or flag as excluded) any permutation with `coverage.get("verify_failed")` regardless of the resulting verdict (PASS or INDETERMINATE), since verify_failed signals an incomplete Pass-2 run irrespective of whether blocking criteria were in scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the AC6 proving command to verify each of the seven enumerated content requirements with distinct, anchored grep patterns (e.g. `grep -q 'self_consistency.*pass\|order_shuffle.*pass'` for gate-input verdicts, `grep -q 'LLM.*spend\|agentic.*call\|150\|42'` for spend figures) or accept that the grep is illustrative and explicitly note it is not exhaustive.
evidence: 'grep -Eq \'criterion.*(G6|E4|T3)\'' — matches any line containing 'criterion' followed anywhere by 'G6', 'E4', or 'T3', including incidental references
'grep -Eq \'kappa|Fleiss\'' — a single word in a table header satisfies this

The AC lists seven distinct content requirements (cohort filter, N/counts, Exp B sample, permutation rule, metric definitions+floors, prompts-as-run reference, LLM-spend, two gate-input verdicts) but the proving command only spot-checks three patterns and could green-light a README missing four of the seven
findings.finding: The AC6 writeup proving command uses shallow grep patterns (`'criterion.*(G6|E4|T3)'`, `'kappa|Fleiss'`, `'corpus_sample'`) that would pass if these strings appear anywhere in the file — including in a comment, a code block, or an unrelated sentence — without verifying that the required substantive documentation is actually present (cohort filter, metric definitions, permutation rule, LLM-spend figures, gate-input verdicts for R3/R5).
findings.location: AC 'Writeup records provenance for R3 and R5' — Proving: `grep -Eq ...` command
scenarios: A README that mentions 'kappa' once in passing and lists 'G6' in a bullet but omits the bounded LLM-spend figures, the container-requirement rationale, or the R3/R5 gate-input verdicts satisfies the grep but fails the AC's stated intent

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: robust
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the AC 3 prove script to assert that for each plan the permutation with `permutation_index==0` has `section_order` equal to the ascending identity (e.g., `assert all(r['section_order']==list(range(len(r['section_order']))) for r in ins if r['permutation_index']==0)`) so the identity-first invariant is machine-checked.
evidence: What/Exp B: 'permutation 0 is the identity (the plan's original section order)'
What/Exp B: 'the concrete section_order (an index list) of each of the 3 is committed … so the experiment is re-runnable without re-deriving the shuffle'
AC 3 prove: 'assert len(byp)>=14 and all(len(s)==3 for s in byp.values())' — only checks count of distinct permutations, not identity at index 0
findings.finding: The What section explicitly defines that 'permutation 0 is the identity (the plan's original section order)' as a key reproducibility property of Exp B, and states 'the concrete section_order (an index list) of each of the 3 is committed'. However, the AC 3 prove script only checks that each plan has 3 *distinct* permutations (`len(s)==3`); it does not verify that `permutation_index==0` corresponds to the identity ordering (i.e., `section_order == list(range(n_sections))`). An implementer could commit 3 arbitrary distinct permutations and pass all ACs while violating this reproducibility invariant.
findings.location: What / Exp B Mechanism section vs. AC 3 (Exp B run recorded) prove script
scenarios: Implementer commits 3 arbitrary distinct shuffles for each plan; AC 3 passes; but permutation 0 is not the original order, breaking the 'original vs. shuffled' comparison and making the result irreproducible from the plan spec alone.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC 3 prove script: `assert all(r['section_order']==list(range(len(r['section_order']))) for r in ins if r['permutation_index']==0), 'permutation 0 must be identity'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 1/3) that verifies `e6_judge_reliability.py` exists and is importable, e.g. `python3 -c "import importlib.util; s=importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('e6','docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py'); assert s is not None"; echo OK` — ensuring the driver is a committed, loadable artifact.
evidence: Scope: 'docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py — the driver: builds frozen inputs (Exp A snapshot + Exp B permutations), runs the votes/permutations, and writes the agreement tables + summary'
No AC prove command references or imports e6_judge_reliability.py or checks its existence
AC 4 proves test_e6_agreement.py (pure helpers only); ACs 1/2/3/4 prove runs/* output files
findings.finding: The Scope names `e6_judge_reliability.py` as a primary deliverable ('the driver: builds frozen inputs, runs the votes/permutations, and writes the agreement tables + summary'), but no AC verifies that this file exists, is runnable, or produces the committed outputs. ACs 1–4 only verify the output files in `runs/` and the unit-test file — an implementer could satisfy all ACs with manually crafted output files, leaving the harness absent or broken.
findings.location: Scope section (e6_judge_reliability.py entry) vs. ACs 1–4
scenarios: A reviewer satisfies all ACs by hand-crafting the JSONL/CSV/JSON outputs without a reusable harness; E6 cannot be re-run to validate R3/R5 decisions.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: `python3 -c "import importlib.util, pathlib; p=pathlib.Path('docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py'); assert p.exists(), f'harness missing: {p}'; print('harness present')"` as a fast structural check that the driver was actually committed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete values (or config-read recipe) for `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` in the Exp A harness spec — e.g. 'read from `gate_config.block_threshold` via `config.assert_gated()`' — so the executor does not have to investigate.
evidence: Literal `...` (ellipsis) in two parameter positions of the only code call to `pass3_decide` in the experiment spec
No prose in the plan specifies what values these take, or instructs the executor to read them from `config.assert_gated` or any other source

The plan says 'Pass-3 is deterministic given Pass-2, so all measured variance is the judge's Pass-2 stochasticity' — but this only holds if the same threshold config is used consistently, which requires knowing what config to use
findings.finding: The Exp A harness code snippet passes `block_threshold=...` and `blocking_enabled=...` as literal Python ellipsis to `pass3_decide`, with no guidance on what values to supply, where to read them from (gate config, a constant, a CLI flag), or what the production defaults are. Since these parameters determine whether a finding is classified `block` vs `advisory`, using wrong values would make the measured self-consistency distribution diverge from the real gate's behavior — yet no AC verifies that the thresholds match production config.
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run code snippet: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v["verify"], block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
scenarios: Executor uses incorrect block_threshold and all findings are classified `advisory` regardless of severity — self-consistency appears artificially high (κ→1.0) because every vote lands in the same bucket
Executor picks arbitrary thresholds that differ from the gate's production values — the reliability measurement no longer reflects the real gate's behavior, undermining the R5 gate claim
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Pass `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` from the live gate config: `cfg = config.assert_gated(); three_pass.pass3_decide(v["verify"], block_threshold=cfg.block_threshold, blocking_enabled=cfg.blocking_enabled)` — this ensures the thresholds match the production gate.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the throwaway REBAR_TRACKER_DIR clone's full lifecycle: specify that the harness creates a fresh `tempfile.mkdtemp`-based clone **per plan** at the start of that plan's permutation loop, wraps the 3 permutation runs in a `try/finally` that calls `shutil.rmtree` on the clone, and records the clone path so a crash-recovery re-run can detect and remove stale clones before starting.
evidence: Plan says 'set REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<tmp clone of the tickets store>' with no explanation of who creates the clone or when
Plan says 'Only the throwaway REBAR_TRACKER_DIR clone is edited — the real tickets store is never written' but says nothing about post-run or post-crash cleanup
Exp B runs 14 × 3 = 42 permutations; if 1-clone-per-plan, the same clone is edited 3 times; if 1-clone-per-permutation, 42 clones may accumulate — neither is specified
ABSENCE: no `shutil.rmtree` / `finally` / temp-directory-context equivalent mentioned
findings.finding: The throwaway REBAR_TRACKER_DIR store clone used in Exp B has no defined creation, scope, or retirement/cleanup path. The plan says 'set REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<tmp clone>' and 'real store untouched' but never specifies: who creates the clone (harness? pre-step?), whether it is one clone per plan or one per permutation, and — critically — whether it is cleaned up on success or on crash. A crash mid-experiment leaves a mutated clone that could corrupt a subsequent re-run, and there is no retirement path specified.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section; the three-step execution recipe block (`set REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<tmp clone>` / `rebar edit` / `review_plan`)
scenarios: Harness crashes after permutation 1 of plan X: the clone exists with permutation-1's description written, causing the next run to start from a wrong state rather than the original
Multiple experiment runs accumulate unreferenced clone directories if cleanup is not specified
If one clone per plan is reused across 3 permutations, concurrent or interleaved runs could corrupt each other's description writes
findings.suggested_fix: In the Exp B mechanism section, add: 'The harness creates a fresh temporary directory (e.g. `tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="e6_shuffle_")`) as the per-plan REBAR_TRACKER_DIR clone by copying the real tracker dir at the start of each plan's 3-permutation loop, and unconditionally deletes it in a `finally` block after all 3 permutations are recorded. The real tracker dir is never touched.' Also specify that if the harness is restarted, it scans for stale `e6_shuffle_*` directories and removes them before proceeding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete `block_threshold` and `blocking_enabled` values to pass to `pass3_decide` in the Exp A harness snippet — either state 'use the criterion's `block_threshold`/`default_posture` from `criteria_v8.json`' (matching `run_three_pass` behaviour) or justify using the function defaults, and update the snippet accordingly.
evidence: Plan quote: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v["verify"], block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
three_pass.py:283 — `def pass3_decide(verify, block_threshold=0.95, blocking_enabled=False)` — defaults exist but per-criterion dispatch reads criterion posture
three_pass.py:355-366 — `run_three_pass` reads `postures = {c['id']: c.get('default_posture','advisory')...}` and `thresholds = {c['id']: c.get('block_threshold', 0.95)...}` per criterion

The adjudication_corpus.jsonl rows for G6/E4/T3 carry no `default_posture` or `block_threshold` field — the values must come from the criteria_v8.json rubric, which the plan never instructs the harness to load
findings.finding: The Exp A harness code snippet passes `block_threshold=...` and `blocking_enabled=...` as Python Ellipsis literals to `pass3_decide`, with no specification of what concrete values to supply. The function has defaults (0.95/False) but the plan's text requires per-criterion dispatch (reading `default_posture` and `block_threshold` from the criterion record), exactly as `run_three_pass` does at three_pass.py:355-366. An executor implementing the harness cannot determine whether to use the function defaults or to look up per-criterion values from the corpus row or rubric — these produce materially different `decision` outcomes (especially for the block path).
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run section, harness code snippet: `d = three_pass.pass3_decide(v["verify"], block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...)`
scenarios: Executor uses defaults (0.95/False): every cohort finding yields 'advisory' or 'dropped', never 'block', even if the criterion is blocking-enabled — distorting the agreement distribution.
Executor looks up rubric: must independently discover criteria_v8.json path and lookup logic not described in the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `block_threshold=..., blocking_enabled=...` with `block_threshold=RUBRIC_THRESHOLDS.get(inp['criterion'], 0.95), blocking_enabled=(RUBRIC_POSTURES.get(inp['criterion']) == 'blocking')` and add a line loading `criteria_v8.json` into `RUBRIC_THRESHOLDS`/`RUBRIC_POSTURES` dicts at harness startup.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit exception-level error handling around `pass2_verify` and `review_plan` calls: catch transient LLM errors (network/rate-limit/5xx exceptions) with exponential backoff and retry (≤3 retries per call), and surface hard failures (exhausted retries) as a recorded exclusion row rather than an unhandled exception that aborts the full run.
evidence: Plan's infra exclusion is defined exclusively in terms of `decision == 'indeterminate'` with `v['verify'] is None`, and `coverage.llm_unavailable` / `coverage.verify_failed` on a verdict — no mention of exception handling
Plan states 150 agentic Pass-2 calls and 42 full-gate runs with no try/except or backoff around the LLM calls themselves
ABSENCE: no mention of `tenacity`, `backoff`, `httpx` timeout, or any exception-level retry around `pass2_verify` or `review_plan` calls
findings.finding: Hard LLM API errors (network errors, rate-limit exceptions, 5xx responses that raise exceptions rather than returning an INDETERMINATE verdict) are not handled by the plan's retry logic. The re-run logic only covers the INDETERMINATE verdict path (decision=='indeterminate' from pass3_decide, or coverage.llm_unavailable/verify_failed on a finalize_verdict result). An unhandled exception from the LLM layer would abort the entire harness run, wasting all preceding LLM spend.
findings.location: Experiment A — 'Per-vote infra exclusion' section; Experiment B — 'infra-INDETERMINATE exclusion' mechanism
scenarios: An OpenAI/Anthropic rate-limit (HTTP 429) raises an exception inside pass2_verify after 60 of 150 calls; the entire harness aborts with no checkpoint, losing all 60 votes' worth of LLM spend
A transient network error mid-Exp B permutation raises an unhandled exception, requiring a full restart

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the harness appends each vote/permutation result to the output JSONL file immediately after it is collected (not batched at end), and that on startup the harness reads any existing output file to skip already-completed (finding_id, vote_index) or (plan_id, permutation_index) pairs, enabling crash-safe resume without re-spending LLM budget.
evidence: Plan says the driver 'runs the votes/permutations, and writes the agreement tables + summary' — no mention of append-per-vote vs write-at-end
LLM-spend section explicitly states 150 + 42 = 192 live billable calls with no checkpoint/resume mechanism
ABSENCE: no mention of `jsonlines` append mode, resume-from-checkpoint logic, or run-state persistence between calls
findings.finding: The 192 live LLM calls (150 Exp A + 42 Exp B) are made with no incremental checkpointing: the plan does not specify that results are written to the output JSONL files per-vote/per-permutation (append mode) rather than all-at-end. If the harness crashes after call 100, all progress is lost and the full LLM budget must be re-spent, with no way to resume from the checkpoint.
findings.location: Scope section ('`docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py` — the driver … runs the votes/permutations, and writes the agreement tables + summary'); AC 'Exp A run recorded' and 'Exp B run recorded'
scenarios: Harness crashes at call 140 of 150 in Exp A due to an unhandled exception or OOM; re-running from scratch spends another 150 calls and the prior 140 calls' cost is wasted
A multi-hour Exp B run is interrupted mid-way; with no checkpoint, the operator must restart from plan 1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The harness opens output JSONL files in append mode and flushes one record per completed vote/permutation immediately; on startup it reads any existing output file and skips already-recorded (finding_id, vote_index) or (plan_id, permutation_index) pairs to support crash-safe resume.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa` (or equivalent) is already a project dependency and, if so, delegate to it instead of rolling a bespoke implementation; if not available, document the explicit decision to inline the formula and add a cross-check fixture against a known reference value.
evidence: Plan: 'tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py — CI-collectable golden test for the pure helpers (fleiss_kappa, raw_agreement, permute_sections) on fixed fixtures; no LLM.'
Search for 'fleiss_kappa' across entire codebase: zero hits — no existing implementation or import.
Search for 'import statsmodels', 'import scipy', 'import numpy' across codebase: zero hits — neither statsmodels nor any numeric library is in use.
statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa is a standard, well-tested implementation of exactly this metric.
findings.finding: The plan specifies bespoke `fleiss_kappa` and `raw_agreement` helper functions that will live in a CI-collected unit-test file (`tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py`), without justifying why a purpose-built implementation is preferred over `statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa` (a well-known existing library function). Fleiss' κ has a non-trivial formula (especially the expected-agreement term); rolling it from scratch for production CI use when a maintained library exists is a NIH risk. The plan does not confirm whether `statsmodels` is already a project dependency or intentionally excluded.
findings.location: Scope section — `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py`; Exp A metric section; Exp B metric section
scenarios: The bespoke implementation contains a subtle formula error (e.g. wrong denominator for expected agreement under unequal category marginals), producing κ values that pass the unit tests but are numerically wrong — silently corrupting the R3/R5 gate decision.
statsmodels is already available in the environment but the plan adds a parallel implementation, creating two divergent sources of truth.
findings.suggested_fix: If statsmodels is available: `from statsmodels.stats.inter_rater import fleiss_kappa` and wrap it; if not, inline the formula with a docstring citing the reference and add a fixture that cross-checks against the Wang et al. (2024) reported κ values to catch formula errors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the library or cite the exact formula (e.g. `statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa` or the Fleiss 1971 formula) that will implement `fleiss_kappa`, and confirm the dependency is declared (or will be added) in the repo's experiment dependency set.
evidence: Plan names `fleiss_kappa` as a pure helper to be unit-tested in `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py` and used in `e6_judge_reliability.py`, but provides no import path or library reference.
ABSENCE: `.github/release-requirements.in` lists only `build`, `hatchling`, `twine`; `statsmodels`, `scipy`, and `sklearn` do not appear in repo dependency files.

Multi-category Fleiss' κ (4 decision categories, fixed 3 raters) is a non-trivial formula; a hand-rolled implementation without citing a reference or a library risks an off-by-one in the category-marginal computation.
findings.finding: The plan requires a `fleiss_kappa` function (used in the harness and unit-tested with hand-computed fixtures) but never declares which library or implementation provides it, and no dependency that supplies it (e.g. `statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa`, `sklearn`, or `scipy`) appears in the repo's declared dependency stack.
findings.location: Scope section — `tests/unit/test_e6_agreement.py` / `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/harnesses/e6_judge_reliability.py`; AC 'Pure agreement/permutation helpers are unit-tested'
scenarios: The harness is run and `fleiss_kappa` silently uses a subtly wrong formula (e.g. treating it as Cohen's κ), producing a plausible-looking but incorrect κ that leads to an incorrect PASS/FAIL gate decision for R3 or R5.
A reviewer implementing the unit test cannot reproduce the known-κ fixture because the formula used differs from the standard Fleiss (1971) definition.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the Scope section: 'The `fleiss_kappa` helper uses `statsmodels.stats.inter_rater.fleiss_kappa` (or a hand-rolled implementation of the Fleiss 1971 formula verified against the known-κ fixture in the unit test). `statsmodels` is added as a dev/experiment dependency.' Alternatively, since raters=3 is fixed, note that the multi-category κ reduces to a simple formula and include it inline.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete `e6_summary.json` schema definition (as a JSON example or table) to the Scope or a dedicated 'Output schema' subsection, specifying all required top-level keys and the complete structure of `self_consistency` and `order_shuffle` sub-objects.
evidence: AC2 proving script: `d=json.load(open('...e6_summary.json'))['self_consistency']; assert d['n_findings']>=50 and set(('fleiss_kappa','raw_agreement','pass'))<=set(d)`
AC4 proving script: `d=json.load(open('...e6_summary.json'))['order_shuffle']; assert d['n_plans']>=14 and set(('fleiss_kappa','raw_agreement','pass'))<=set(d)`
The plan never specifies whether the file also contains metadata keys (run_date, cohort_filter, seed, llm_spend, etc.) or only these two sub-objects

The writeup AC (AC5) says the README records 'the two gate-input verdicts (self_consistency.pass → R5, order_shuffle.pass → R3)' — implying these are in e6_summary.json — but the full schema is still unspecified
findings.finding: The `e6_summary.json` output file is referenced in four separate AC proving scripts and named as the authoritative gate output, but its schema (top-level keys, the structure of `self_consistency` and `order_shuffle` sub-objects beyond the fields tested in the proving snippets, whether other keys are required) is never defined anywhere in the plan. An implementer writing `e6_judge_reliability.py` must invent the schema, which risks a mismatch with what the proving scripts expect.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Exp A passes its floor (AC2) and Exp B passes its floor (AC4) proving scripts, and Scope section: `docs/experiments/plan-review-gate/runs/`
scenarios: Implementer writes e6_summary.json with different key names (e.g. `kappa` instead of `fleiss_kappa`) and the proving script silently passes the set-membership check but with wrong values.
A downstream consumer (R3/R5 gating logic) reads a key that the implementer didn't include.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `permute_sections`'s call signature and return type explicitly in the Experiment B mechanism section (e.g., `permute_sections(plan_text: str, plan_id: str, n: int = 3) -> list[str]` returning permuted plan strings, with `section_order` recorded separately as the index list used for each permutation).
evidence: Plan quote: 'a pure helper `permute_sections` generates exactly 3 distinct section-order permutations by reordering the plan's top-level `##` section blocks'
AC1 proving script references `section_order` as a committed field in `e6_ordershuffle_inputs.jsonl`: `byp[r['plan_id']].add(tuple(r['section_order']))`
The plan never specifies: does `permute_sections` take a plan string and return 3 plan strings? Or does it return 3 index lists? Does it take a `plan_id` or a `plan` dict from corpus_sample.json?

The `SEED = int(plan_id.split('-')[0], 16)` formula assumes `plan_id` is a hex-prefixed UUID — but corpus_sample.json uses ids like 'b744-4fb9-2d05-4b49' (verified in the file), making `int('b744', 16) = 46916` a valid seed; this works but is never stated as the return contract
findings.finding: The `permute_sections` helper is named as a pure function to be unit-tested in `test_e6_agreement.py` and used in the Exp B mechanism, but its call signature (input type: raw plan string? structured dict? which field?) and return type (list of permuted plan strings? list of index lists? something else?) are never defined. The AC unit-test specification says it 'returns exactly 3 distinct orderings with permutation 0 == identity, is reproducible under the pinned per-plan seed, and preserves every section's content while only reordering the `##` blocks' — but without a defined interface, an executor cannot write the function or its tests without inventing the contract.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section: 'a pure helper `permute_sections` generates exactly 3 distinct section-order permutations'; AC5 (unit test): '`permute_sections` returns exactly 3 distinct orderings…'
scenarios: Implementer returns permuted plan strings; unit test compares strings. Another implementer returns index lists and reconstructs; both pass the AC text but produce different `section_order` fields in the jsonl.
The `test_e6_agreement.py` test cannot be written without knowing the function signature.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback/verification step after `rebar edit` in the Exp B recipe: either assert the written description matches the permuted text (e.g., `rebar show <ticket_id>` and compare) before calling `review_plan`, or abort and report the permutation as failed rather than silently proceeding.
evidence: Plan quote: '2. write the permuted plan as the ticket's description: `rebar edit <ticket_id> --description=<permuted>`'
Plan gives no try/except, no verification step, no re-run protocol for this step
The plan specifies infra-INDETERMINATE re-run for step 3 (`review_plan`) but is silent on step 2 failure
A failed write would cause `review_plan` to read the original description, producing a false agreement result
findings.finding: The Exp B store-clone recipe (step 2: `rebar edit <ticket_id> --description=<permuted>`) has no defined fallback when the edit command fails. The plan notes 'real store untouched' and 'non-mutating' for the real store, but gives no error-handling protocol for a failure in the throwaway clone write — which would silently run `review_plan` against the unmodified (identity) description, producing a duplicate of permutation 0 rather than the intended permuted version, corrupting the per-plan agreement measurement without any error signal.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section, step 2: `rebar edit <ticket_id> --description=<permuted>`
scenarios: The throwaway store clone's `.tickets-tracker` lock is held by a previous run, causing `rebar edit` to fail silently or with an error that the harness does not check.
The permuted description exceeds a field-length limit in the store, causing the edit to fail.
findings.suggested_fix: After step 2, add: `3a. verify: assert rebar_show(ticket_id, tracker_dir=tmp_clone).description == permuted_text, 'edit failed'; else skip this permutation and log as excluded (not re-run with wrong text).'`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `criteria` (the plural array field, e.g. `["G6"]`) to the Exp A frozen input schema spec for `e6_selfconsistency_inputs.jsonl`, so `pass2_verify`'s agentic dispatch condition (`finding.get('criteria', [])` at `three_pass.py:185`) correctly routes cohort findings to the agentic path.
evidence: Plan schema for `e6_selfconsistency_inputs.jsonl`: `{finding_id, ticket_id, title, plan, criterion, finding, location, suggested_fix}` — `criterion` listed, `criteria` omitted.
`three_pass.py:185`: `grounded = agentic and repo_root and any(c in CODEBASE_GROUNDED for c in finding.get('criteria', []))` — uses `criteria` (plural).
If `criteria` key is absent, `finding.get('criteria', [])` returns `[]`, `any(...)` is False, and `_verify_agentic` is never called.

The `adjudication_corpus.jsonl` DOES have both `criterion` and `criteria` fields (verified at line 1: `"criterion": "G6", "criteria": ["G6"]`), so the build step could copy it — but the plan's frozen-input schema specification does not list `criteria`.
findings.finding: The Exp A frozen input schema (`e6_selfconsistency_inputs.jsonl`) is specified as `{finding_id, ticket_id, title, plan, criterion, finding, location, suggested_fix}` — using `criterion` (singular) but omitting `criteria` (plural array). However, `pass2_verify` at `three_pass.py:185` dispatches to the agentic path via `finding.get('criteria', [])` (the plural key). If frozen inputs are built without the `criteria` field, the dispatch condition `any(c in CODEBASE_GROUNDED for c in finding.get('criteria', []))` evaluates to False for every row, silently routing all votes through single-turn instead of the agentic path — contradicting the plan's assertion that 'every cohort finding takes the agentic path.'
findings.location: Experiment A — 'Judge run' section; frozen input schema spec `{finding_id, ticket_id, title, plan, criterion, finding, location, suggested_fix}`; `three_pass.py:185`
scenarios: The Exp A harness build step copies only the fields listed in the plan's schema, omitting `criteria`. All 150 votes then run single-turn (not agentic), making the self-consistency measurement irrelevant to the agentic verifier that R5 depends on.
A reader implementing `e6_judge_reliability.py` from the plan spec alone builds frozen inputs with only `criterion`, causing silent fallback to single-turn for every vote.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the frozen input schema to `{finding_id, ticket_id, title, plan, criterion, criteria, finding, location, suggested_fix}` where `criteria` is copied from the `adjudication_corpus.jsonl` row's `criteria[]` field. This ensures `pass2_verify(inp, agentic=True, repo_root=...)` dispatches to `_verify_agentic` for all G6/E4/T3 findings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For Exp B (N=14), supplement the κ≥0.6 hard gate with a reported 95% confidence interval for κ, and either (a) increase N to ≥30 by including the 5 sub-3-section plans with a relaxed inclusion criterion, or (b) explicitly acknowledge in the writeup that N=14 yields wide CI and the gate is directional only.
evidence: Plan: 'Over 14 plans × 3 permutations, compute Fleiss' κ and raw exact-agreement on the verdict categories. PASS iff κ ≥ 0.6 AND raw agreement ≥ 0.8'
Plan acknowledges κ deflation under skewed base rates for Exp A but does not mention variance/power issues for N=14 in Exp B.
Statistical literature: Fleiss' κ SE ≈ sqrt(2/(N*k*(k-1))) for balanced designs; at N=14, k=3 this is ~0.15 per category, giving CI width >0.3.
No power analysis or confidence-interval reporting is specified in the ACs.
findings.finding: The plan applies Fleiss' κ as a hard pass/fail gate with an identical κ≥0.6 floor to both Exp A (N=50 findings) and Exp B (N=14 plans). Fleiss' κ is statistically unstable at N=14 — with only 14 subjects the 95% confidence interval for κ spans roughly ±0.25–0.35, meaning a true κ of 0.55 could easily sample above 0.6 (false PASS) or a true κ of 0.65 could sample below (false FAIL). Applying the same threshold without a power caveat or confidence-interval report is a cargo-cult use of κ — copying the metric from the N=50 context where it is appropriate, without checking whether it is informative at N=14.
findings.location: Exp B — Metric + floor section; AC 'Exp B passes its floor'
scenarios: The gate produces a PASS verdict at κ=0.62 on the 14-plan sample, R3 proceeds, but the true underlying κ is 0.50 (unreliable); the experiment has given a false confidence signal.
The gate produces a FAIL verdict at κ=0.58 on the 14-plan sample, blocking R3, when the true κ is 0.70 (reliable); R3 is incorrectly delayed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the Exp B mechanism that the harness must set `os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR']` to the temp clone path in-process (not just as a shell step) before each `review_plan` call and restore it after, and that permutations must be processed sequentially (or use per-permutation subprocess isolation or a thread-local store-path override) to avoid global env-var races.
evidence: In gate_source.gate_read_root (gate_source.py:104-117), local mode calls only gate_session() — use_tickets_root is NOT called.
LLMConfig.from_env (config.py:599): tickets_path = current_tickets_root() which is None in local mode.
runner.py:243: ticket tools resolve via cfg.tickets_path or cfg.repo_path → REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT in local mode.
config.py:119-136: tracker_dir(REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT) reads .tickets-tracker under the real repo root, not the temp clone, unless REBAR_TRACKER_DIR is set in os.environ.
Plan step 1 says 'set REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<tmp clone>' — this reads as a shell assignment, not as a Python `os.environ` mutation scoped to the review_plan call.
findings.finding: The Exp B mechanism relies on `REBAR_TRACKER_DIR` being active **in the Python process** during the `review_plan` call, but the plan presents this as a shell-level env step. In `local` mode, `gate_read_root` never calls `use_tickets_root`, so `cfg.tickets_path=None`; ticket reads fall back to `tracker_dir(REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT)`, which resolves the REAL store — not the temp clone — unless `os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR']` is set in-process at call time. As written, the store mutation via `rebar edit` into the clone is invisible to `review_plan`, defeating the experiment. An additional edge: if permutations are processed concurrently, global `os.environ` mutation is not thread-safe.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section: 'set REBAR_TRACKER_DIR=<tmp clone of the tickets store>' / 'rebar edit <ticket_id> --description=<permuted>' / 'verdict = review_plan(ticket_id, source="local", ...)'
scenarios: Harness sets REBAR_TRACKER_DIR only for the `rebar edit` subprocess (shell scoping), then calls `review_plan` in the same Python process without setting os.environ — review_plan reads the unmodified real ticket, so all 3 permutations get identical descriptions and the order-shuffle test trivially shows 100% agreement, falsely confirming stability.

Harness sets os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR'] globally but then processes multiple permutations concurrently — concurrent threads see each other's temp-clone paths, corrupting ticket reads mid-experiment.
findings.suggested_fix:
In the execution recipe pseudocode, replace the shell-step framing with explicit Python:
```python
import os, contextlib

@contextlib.contextmanager
def tracker_override(path):
    old = os.environ.get('REBAR_TRACKER_DIR')
    os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR'] = str(path)
    try:
        yield
    finally:
        if old is None: del os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR']
        else: os.environ['REBAR_TRACKER_DIR'] = old

for perm in permutations:  # process SEQUENTIALLY to avoid env-var races
    with tracker_override(tmp_clone_path):
        _write_permuted_plan(tmp_clone_path, ticket_id, perm['plan_text'])
        verdict = review_plan(ticket_id, source='local', repo_root=REAL_REPO_CHECKOUT,
                              sign=False, emit_sidecar=False, force=True)
```
Also note in the mechanism that concurrent permutation processing requires per-process isolation (e.g., subprocess) rather than threading, due to `os.environ` being process-global.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale in the Exp B mechanism section explaining why the public `review_plan` entrypoint (via store clone) is used rather than an in-process plan-injection helper — specifically that exercising the full public entry path is required to capture any store-assembly effects on the plan text that could affect the verdict.
evidence: The plan states the mechanism (store clone + REBAR_TRACKER_DIR + rebar edit + review_plan) but does not explain WHY the public entrypoint must be exercised rather than an in-process plan-injection approach.
No 'why this approach' rationale appears in the What, Scope, or AC sections for Exp B's store-mutation mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for why the store-clone + env-var mutation approach was chosen for Exp B over a simpler alternative (e.g., calling an in-process helper that constructs the PlanContext with the permuted plan text directly, bypassing ticket-store reads entirely). The positive rationale for the chosen mechanism is absent.
findings.location: Experiment B — Mechanism section; Scope section ('Exp B drives the public rebar.llm.plan_review.review_plan entrypoint')
scenarios: An implementer, encountering the env-var scoping complexity, seeks a simpler approach (direct in-process plan injection) and is not equipped by the plan to evaluate whether that would compromise the experiment's validity.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether Exp A should measure the harness `three_pass.pass2_verify` (a separate, retained experimental path) or the production gate's workflow-driven Pass-2 (via `gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict` / `review_plan`); if the intent is to gate R5's production judge, replace the Exp A execution recipe with the same entrypoint Exp B uses (`review_plan`) run on frozen per-finding plan inputs, or explicitly document that the harness is the intended measurement target and explain why it is a valid proxy for the production gate.
evidence: Plan: 'E6 re-judges … for self-consistency … [measuring] Pass-2 verify → Pass-3 decide → orchestrator.finalize_verdict'
passes.py:534: 'the bespoke pass2_verify was retired in epic solid-timer-unison WS1' — the production gate no longer calls three_pass.pass2_verify
Exp A harness snippet calls three_pass.pass2_verify directly, not the production gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict
Partially-verified: the harness pass2_verify exists at three_pass.py:183 and is callable, but it is a different code path from the production gate's workflow-driven verifier
findings.finding: Exp A claims to measure 'the Pass-2 verify → Pass-3 decide' path of 'the existing gate' to gate R5, but the implementation calls three_pass.pass2_verify (a harness function) whose production counterpart was retired (passes.py:534: 'the bespoke pass2_verify was retired in epic solid-timer-unison WS1'). The production gate's Pass-2 now runs through the workflow engine (gate_dispatch.produce_plan_review_verdict → gates/plan-review.yaml → the plan-review-verifier prompt step), a different model-call path. Exp A therefore measures harness judge reliability rather than production-gate judge reliability — a different judge surface.
findings.location: Experiment A — Judge run section; plan prose 'E6 is measurement over the existing gate (Pass-2 verify → Pass-3 decide → orchestrator.finalize_verdict)'; code invocation of three_pass.pass2_verify
scenarios: Exp A passes (κ ≥ 0.6) but the production gate's workflow-verifier is unstable — R5 ships on a false reliability signal.
Exp A fails — R5 is blocked for instability that exists only in the retired harness path, not in the production gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Exp A per-vote infra exclusion section that `coverage` is a `finalize_verdict` / Exp B concept only; state explicitly that Exp A's sole per-vote exclusion trigger is `decision == 'indeterminate'` (i.e., `pass3_decide` returned `{"decision": "indeterminate", "reason": "no-verification"}`) and that no `coverage` dict check is applicable at the per-vote level.
evidence: Plan text in Exp A infra-exclusion section: '`finalize_verdict` records *why* a verdict is INDETERMINATE on the verdict's `coverage` record: an INDETERMINATE carrying `coverage.get("llm_unavailable")` … is an **execution failure**'
This `coverage` explanation appears in the Exp A section header (Grounded definitions) immediately before the Exp A per-vote rule
three_pass.py:283-318: `pass3_decide` return dict has keys `{decision, reason, confidence, severity, impact_ord, blast, likelihood}` — no `coverage` key
orchestrator.py:467-512: `coverage` is a parameter to `finalize_verdict` and is set on the returned verdict dict — this is Exp B's full-gate path
The Exp A per-vote rule correctly uses `decision == 'indeterminate'` but the surrounding coverage explanation implies a `coverage` check that cannot work at the per-vote level
findings.finding: The plan's Exp A 'per-vote infra exclusion' section references `coverage.get("llm_unavailable")` and `coverage.get("verify_failed")` in the same prose block that describes per-vote `pass3_decide` exclusion, but `coverage` is a `finalize_verdict` output field (Exp B's full-gate path) and does not exist in the `pass3_decide` return dict (which only has `decision`, `reason`, `confidence`, `severity`, `impact_ord`, `blast`, `likelihood`). This conflation creates a pink-elephant risk: an implementer reading the Exp A exclusion text may attempt to check `coverage` on the per-vote result — a key that will always be absent — rather than checking `decision == 'indeterminate'` as the plan correctly states one sentence earlier.
findings.location: Experiment A — Per-vote infra exclusion section: 'dropping and re-running any vote with `decision == "indeterminate"` … `coverage.get("llm_unavailable")`/`coverage.get("verify_failed")`'
scenarios: Implementer writes `if vote.get('coverage', {}).get('llm_unavailable'): drop_and_rerun()` — this check always evaluates to False since `pass3_decide` never sets `coverage`, so infra failures are never excluded from Exp A, silently including errored votes in the agreement calculation.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the `coverage.get("llm_unavailable")`/`coverage.get("verify_failed")` explanation entirely into the Exp B mechanism section where it belongs, and rewrite the Exp A per-vote exclusion as: 'Drop and re-run any vote where `pass3_decide` returns `decision == "indeterminate"` (its sole return path when `v["verify"]` is None — three_pass.py:290-293). No `coverage` dict exists at this level.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
